payload.formula_text:
Avg Open Rate = SUM(Unique Opens) / SUM(Recipients) x 100 per Trigger Type
Avg Click Rate = SUM(Unique Clicks) / SUM(Recipients) x 100 per Trigger Type
payload.formula_explanation: Calculates volume-weighted average engagement rates for each trigger type. This accounts for flows of different sizes, giving larger flows proportionally more influence on the average.
how_to_read: Blue bars show Open Rate, green bars show Click Rate for each trigger type
Taller bars indicate better engagement for that metric
Compare the gap between Open and Click rates within each trigger type
Trigger types with high opens but low clicks may have content issues
Use this to identify which trigger types engage your audience best
decisions_answered: Which trigger types produce the highest engagement rates?
Are certain trigger types better for opens vs clicks?
Where is there opportunity to improve content (high opens, low clicks)?
Which automation strategies engage our audience most effectively?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart compares engagement rates across different trigger types.

The formula calculates volume-weighted averages: total unique opens divided by total recipients for Open Rate, same approach for Click Rate. This means larger flows have more influence on the average, which is appropriate.

Blue bars show Open Rate, green bars show Click Rate. Compare them side by side within each trigger type.

The insight is in the patterns. Which trigger types get the best engagement? Is there a trigger type with great opens but poor clicks - that signals content needs work even though the timing and targeting are right.

This chart answers: Which triggers produce the best engagement? Are certain triggers better for opens versus clicks? And which automation strategies truly connect with your audience?

payload.formula_text:
Open Rate = Unique Opens / Recipients x 100
Click Rate = Unique Clicks / Recipients x 100
Bounce Rate = Bounces / Sent x 100
payload.formula_explanation: This table consolidates all key metrics for each flow. Green values indicate performance meeting or exceeding targets (Open Rate >= 25%, Click Rate >= 3%). Yellow indicates warning range. Red indicates underperformance requiring attention.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank flows on that metric
Green text indicates strong performance meeting targets
Yellow text indicates acceptable but improvable performance
Red text highlights metrics requiring immediate attention
Click any row to see detailed flow step analysis
decisions_answered: Which flows are underperforming and need optimization?
How do different flows compare across all key metrics?
Which flows have deliverability issues (high bounce rates)?
Where should we focus optimization efforts for maximum impact?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows detailed performance metrics for every active flow in your Klaviyo account.

The key formulas: Open Rate is unique opens divided by recipients. Click Rate is unique clicks divided by recipients. Bounce Rate is bounces divided by emails sent.

Color coding helps you spot issues quickly. Green means the metric meets targets - Open Rate at or above 25%, Click Rate at or above 3%. Red means underperformance that needs attention.

You can sort by any column to find your best and worst performers. Click any row to drill into that specific flow.

Use this table to answer: Which flows need optimization? How do flows compare across metrics? Which have deliverability problems? And where should you focus your improvement efforts for the biggest impact?

payload.formula_text:
Open Rate = (Unique Opens / Recipients) x 100
Click Rate = (Unique Clicks / Recipients) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Both rates measure unique engagement - each recipient is counted only once regardless of how many times they open or click. This gives a truer picture of audience engagement than raw event counts.
how_to_read: Green line tracks weekly Open Rate - the percentage of recipients who opened emails
Blue line tracks weekly Click Rate - the percentage who clicked links in emails
Upward trends indicate improving engagement, downward trends signal potential issues
Look for the gap between Open and Click rates - a wide gap may indicate subject line success but content issues
Sudden drops often correlate with deliverability issues or list quality problems
decisions_answered: Are our flow engagement rates improving or declining over time?
Which weeks showed exceptional or poor performance that warrant investigation?
Is there a seasonal pattern in our flow engagement?
Are recent flow optimizations showing measurable impact?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks your Klaviyo flow engagement over the past 90 days, broken down by week.

The formula is straightforward: Open Rate equals unique opens divided by recipients, times 100. Click Rate uses the same formula with unique clicks.

The green line shows Open Rate - what percentage of recipients opened your flow emails. The blue line shows Click Rate - what percentage clicked through.

Watch for trends. Rising lines mean better engagement. Falling lines signal problems to investigate. Pay attention to the gap between open and click rates - if opens are high but clicks are low, your subject lines are working but your content may need improvement.

Use this chart to answer: Are engagement rates improving? Which weeks performed best or worst? And are your recent optimizations actually working?

payload.formula_text: Total Recipients = SUM(Unique Recipients per Flow) grouped by Trigger Type
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates all recipients across flows that share the same trigger type. This shows which automation triggers reach the most people in your audience.
how_to_read: Taller bars indicate trigger types reaching more recipients
Common trigger types include: Added to List, Metric, Date, Segment, Price Drop
Hover over each bar to see exact recipient counts
Compare relative bar heights to understand your automation portfolio balance
Missing or small trigger types may represent growth opportunities
decisions_answered: Which trigger types drive the most recipient volume?
Is our automation portfolio balanced across different trigger types?
Are there underutilized trigger types we should expand?
Which trigger mechanisms are most valuable for our business?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart breaks down your flow recipients by trigger type.

The formula simply sums all unique recipients across flows that share the same trigger mechanism - whether that's being added to a list, a metric being triggered, a date condition, or joining a segment.

Taller bars mean more recipients reached through that trigger type. Compare bar heights to understand your automation portfolio.

Are you over-reliant on one trigger type? Are there trigger mechanisms you're underutilizing? For example, if Price Drop triggers are small but your business has frequent price changes, that could be an opportunity.

This chart answers: Which triggers reach the most people? Is your portfolio balanced? And where should you consider building new automations?

payload.formula_text: Recipient Share = (Flow Recipients / Total Recipients across Top 5) x 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows the distribution of recipients among your top 5 highest-volume flows. The center displays the combined total of all top flows, while each slice represents one flow's share.
how_to_read: Each slice represents one flow - larger slices indicate higher recipient volume
Center value shows total recipients across all displayed flows
Hover over any slice to see exact recipient count and percentage
A dominant single slice may indicate over-reliance on one automation
Well-distributed slices suggest a healthy automation portfolio
decisions_answered: Which individual flows reach the most people?
Is recipient volume concentrated in a few flows or well-distributed?
Which flows should be prioritized for optimization given their reach?
Are there high-volume flows that need performance attention?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how recipients are distributed across your top 5 highest-volume flows.

Each slice represents one flow. The size of the slice shows that flow's share of total recipients. The center number is the combined total across all five flows.

What to look for: If one slice dominates - say 70% or more - you're heavily reliant on that single automation. That's risky. If something breaks or performance drops, it impacts most of your flow program.

Ideally, you want reasonable distribution across multiple flows. This chart helps you identify which flows deserve the most optimization attention based purely on reach.

It answers: Which flows reach the most people? Is your volume concentrated or distributed? And which automations should get priority for improvements?
